The PKGBUILD defines a standard binary package fetching from the official upstream GitHub repository (bitrise-io/stepman) over HTTPS. The source arrays point to github.com/${_pkgauthor}/${_pkgname}/..., which is the project's own release assets. Checksums are provided (not SKIP) for all downloads. There is no pkgver() function, so no code executes there. The top-level scope only sets variables and arrays. No eval, obfuscation, or malicious commands are present. Therefore, running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare and makepkg --printsrcinfo will only fetch the specified sources and parse the PKGBUILD, which is safe.
Safe: standard binary PKGBUILD from official GitHub upstream + Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: standard binary PKGBUILD from official GitHub upstream[/] Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent... makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent... makepkg --printsrcinfo successful Reviewing required files... [0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD... [0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, stepman-0.21.1-LICENSE... LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD: SAFE
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a pre-compiled binary package. All source URLs point to the official GitHub repository of the upstream project (bitrise-io/stepman). Checksums are provided and pinned for all source files, including the binary, ensuring integrity. The `package()` function only installs the binary and documentation files into the expected locations. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests, no dangerous commands (eval, curl|bash, etc.), and no file operations outside the package's own scope. No evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.
Standard AUR PKGBUILD with pinned checksums, no malicious code. [1/2] Reviewing stepman-0.21.1-LICENSE... + Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard AUR PKGBUILD with pinned checksums, no malicious code. LLM auditresponse for stepman-0.21.1-LICENSE: SAFE
This file is the MIT License for the stepman project (copyright Bitrise). It contains only standard license text with no executable code, hidden instructions, obfuscation, or any other content that could be interpreted as malicious. There are no network requests, file operations, system modifications, or encoded commands. The file is purely a legal notice and does not pose any security risk.
Safe license file, no malicious content. [2/2] Reviewing ... + Reviewed stepman-0.21.1-LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- Safe license file, no malicious content. Reviewed all required files. Deciding which files to review next... Decided to review 2 additional files: .nvchecker.toml, stepman-0.21.1-README.md Reviewing additional files... Reviewing additional files... [0/2] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml... [0/2] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml, stepman-0.21.1-README.md... LLM auditresponse for stepman-0.21.1-README.md: SAFE
This file is a standard README markdown document for the stepman project. It contains no executable code, no obfuscated commands, no network requests, and no file operations. It only provides a description of the tool, installation instructions (pointing to the official GitHub releases page), and usage guidance. There is no evidence of malicious or suspicious behavior. The content conforms to normal documentation practices for an open-source project.
Standard README, no security concerns. [1/2] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml... + Reviewed stepman-0.21.1-README.md. Status: SAFE -- Standard README, no security concerns. LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml: SAFE
The file is a standard TOML configuration for nvchecker, a tool that automates version checking. It specifies that the package `stepman-bin` should track the latest release of the `bitrise-io/stepman` GitHub repository, with a version prefix of `"v"`. This is a normal, non-executable configuration file with no embedded commands, obfuscation, or suspicious behavior. It does not perform any actions itself; it merely provides data for nvchecker to use. There is no evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious intent.
Benign nvchecker config for upstream tracking. [2/2] Reviewing ... + Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Benign nvchecker config for upstream tracking. Reviewed all additional files. Reviewed additional files. Audit complete! Result: No issues found API Usage Summary Models: deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash Prompt Tokens: 11,404 Completion Tokens: 1,650 Total Tokens: 13,054 Total Cost: $0.001323 Execution Time: 23.57 seconds Full audit report can be found in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-report-stepman-bin.txt Final Status: SAFE No issues found.

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD sources provided are safe to execute under `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The global scope contains only variable assignments and array definitions, with no command substitutions, function calls, or executable code. The `source` array uses standard, direct URLs pointing to the official GitHub repository of the project (https://github.com/bitrise-io/stepman), fetching versioned release binaries and files via HTTPS. All checksums are explicitly defined with matching sha256sums for both architectures, providing integrity verification.

The `pkgver` variable is hard-coded as `0.21.1`, so no dynamic version fetching (e.g., via git or web APIs) is performed. As a result, there is no risk of remote code execution, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network activity during the sourcing phase. The architecture-specific sources are statically declared and reference official release artifacts. No `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` logic is executed under the specified flags, and the top-level scope contains no malicious or suspicious constructs.
</details>
<summary>
No dynamic code or unsafe sourcing
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dynamic code or unsafe sourcing[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, stepman-0.21.1-LICENSE...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches pre-built binary releases of stepman from the official GitHub repository (bitrise-io/stepman) for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The source URLs point directly to versioned release binaries and associated license/README files using the `v${pkgver}` tag, which is a standard and secure practice for binary packages. Checksums (sha256sums) are provided for all downloaded files, including architecture-specific binaries, ensuring integrity verification.

The package function installs the binary into `/usr/bin/` and documentation into standard directories (`/usr/share/doc`, `/usr/share/licenses`), which is consistent with Arch Linux packaging guidelines. There are no suspicious operations such as dynamic downloads during build, execution of external scripts, use of `eval`, or obfuscated commands. All actions are transparent, minimal, and limited to installing verified release assets.

No network operations, file manipulations, or system modifications occur beyond what is expected for a binary package installation. The use of `install` with proper permissions and the absence of post-install hooks or scripts further confirms a low-risk, straightforward packaging approach.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified releases; no malicious activity
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing stepman-0.21.1-LICENSE...

License files do not pose a security risk as they are documentation rather than executable components. This file does not contain any network requests, file operations, or system modifications. It is intended solely to communicate licensing terms and should be included in compliance with the MIT License requirements.
</details>
<summary>
License file only, no executable code present.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.nvchecker.toml` configuration file is used by `nvchecker` to monitor upstream versions of software, typically for package maintenance automation. It configures a single source for "stepman-bin" using GitHub releases from the official `bitrise-io/stepman` repository. The settings `use_latest_release = true` and `prefix = "v"` are standard and indicate that the latest GitHub release tag (with a "v" prefix) should be used to determine the version. There are no network requests to suspicious domains, no executable code, and no system modifications defined in this file.

This file does not execute any commands, perform downloads, or manipulate system resources directly. It is a passive configuration file used solely for version checking. The GitHub source is legitimate and matches the expected upstream for the `stepman-bin` package. No obfuscation, data exfiltration, or unsafe behaviors are present.

Such files are commonly used in AUR and other packaging workflows to automate version updates, and this instance adheres to safe, standard practice.
</details>
<summary>
Valid configuration for upstream version checking; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>
